fact: A safety valve (pressure safety valve, PSV) was removed for maintenance and the permit-to-work paperwork was not communicated to the night shift
source: The Public Inquiry into the Piper Alpha Disaster (Cullen Report), Lord Cullen (1990)

fact: Gas escaped through the open pipe where the valve had been removed and found an ignition source, causing the initial explosion
source: The Public Inquiry into the Piper Alpha Disaster (Cullen Report), Lord Cullen (1990)

fact: Two connected rigs (Tartan and Claymore) continued pumping oil and gas into Piper Alpha through pipelines, feeding the fire, because their managers awaited authorization to shut down
source: Piper Alpha: A Survivor's Story, Ed Punchard (2009); also detailed in the Cullen Report

fact: Survivors jumped approximately 30 metres (around 100 feet) from the platform into the North Sea to escape the fire
source: Fire in the Night (documentary, 2013) and survivor testimonies in the Cullen Report

fact: 167 people died in the Piper Alpha disaster (166 workers plus 1 rescuer from the vessel Sandhaven)
source: The Public Inquiry into the Piper Alpha Disaster (Cullen Report), Lord Cullen (1990)

fact: The root cause was a failure in the permit-to-work handover system — a maintenance note that was not passed between shifts
source: The Public Inquiry into the Piper Alpha Disaster (Cullen Report), Lord Cullen (1990); widely cited in process safety literature
